text: Hi and welcome to This is my Architecture. Today I'm here with Florian from TIS.
Hi Marina.
So Florian, before we start with the whole use case, I would love to know a little bit more about TIS.
Yeah, sure. TIS is a cloud solution to manage corporate payments.
This means we integrate our enterprise customers with their banks,
help them to improve operational efficiencies through streamlined payment processes,
lower risks, manage liquidity and help them to gain strategic advantages through better insights.
So Florian, the architecture that you brought today is about a data lake.
Can you just briefly describe us which business challenges did you solve through this architecture?
Yeah, actually we solved two main business challenges with the data lake.
First, it's about managing our data growth efficiently and cost effective.
And second, it's about making data available to our internal platform services,
to customers and partners in a secure and compliant way.
And actually I can imagine that since TIS is a financial provider service,
privacy and security must be key for you. So how is that being reflected here?
Actually, we follow their basic security pillars such as least privileges, data locality, data isolation and automation.
And what we did is basically to have a multi-account setup where we have different accounts for storing the data in the data lake
and separated them from processing of the data.
Okay.
And for the data, we again have multiple accounts separated by data category for public data, internal data and customer data.
And since you have different data categories and also different accounts, how is it actually that you are securing those?
Is there any access control or permission set of policies that you have on top of it?
Yes, we use Lake Formation, which is based on the GLUE metadata catalog and use the fine-grained permissions from Lake Formation
together with Lake Formation tags to restrict access.
And for example, also to ensure that our data scientists and analysts don't get access to personal identifiable information.
Okay, I see. And this architecture seems to be a little bit complex.
So you must for sure want to avoid some manual errors.
So that's why I was wondering if there is some automation on top of it.
Yeah, sure. We automate our overall infrastructure provisioning through Terraform code.
And in the case of the data lake, we not only provision our infrastructure with Terraform,
we also use Terraform to provide data set descriptors which allow us to describe data products in a consistent way in the data lake.
Okay, I see. And what does this exactly enable to you?
This on the one hand side enables us that we can manage security and compliance of the data products in the data lake consistently.
And on the other hand enables us to build a data marketplace based on the metadata and the information provided by Lake Formation and GLUE.
And I can imagine that this marketplace also enables you to just create new business cases as well, right?
Yeah, actually it allows us that our data scientists and data analysts can access the data lake through the data marketplace,
browse through available data sets, explore the data according to the permissions which are granted to them.
Perfect. So now we have a lot of context, but I'm still lacking the question of how do you ingest the data?
Actually, we ingest the data from two primary sources.
On the one hand side, we ingest data from the internet where we use AWS Lambda triggered by EventBridge
and store the data in the S3 buckets of the data lake.
And on the other hand, we use the database migration service to access the binlog files of the MySQL Aurora server
and also write the files on a continuous space to the S3 bucket.
And GLUE is accessing the data to create the metadata catalog based on the data in S3.
And also with the GLUE workflows and GLUE ETL processes, we then transform and process the data as needed.
Okay, thanks. And I can imagine that this is being handled by a scale, right?
So can you just maybe enlighten us with a couple of stats on how much data you are ingesting nowadays?
Yeah, actually we store roughly 15 terabytes of data in the data lake.
And the data is growing about 500 gigabytes every month.
Okay, so now we have the ingestion part covered. But what about the consumption part?
Yeah, basically the data lake is consumed on the one hand side from the analysts through the data marketplace.
But we also integrate our platform services through an API gateway to actually access services and capabilities of the data lake.
I see. So this architecture seems to be really complete.
But is there anything in your wish list that you would like to implement or improve later on in the future as well?
Yeah, we currently already work on implementing the first machine learning models where we experiment with SageMaker,
we investigate using Macy to automatically identify PII data in our data sets.
And we also make first experiments with the Kinesis family, for example, to ingest our logs from the infrastructure
and from the applications to make in-depth analysis on our logs.
So Florian, thanks a lot for showing us how is it possible to enable security and data products, which make this architecture unique.
It was a pleasure. Thank you.
And thanks a lot for watching. This is my architecture.
